en.extract:
The Eyrean grasswren (Amytornis goyderi) is a small grasswren from the Passerine family Maluridae. This is a cryptically plumaged  and uncommon bird endemic to arid regions of Central Australia. The species was discovered by F.W. Andrews in 1874 around the Macumba River at Lake Eyre, and named after the South Australian Surveyor General George Woodroffe Goyder.


Description

At 14–16.5 cm in length, Amytornis goyderi is the smallest grasswren. It has a deep, finch-like bill. There are some minor differences between sexes, and between populations across the distribution.


Adult male
The head is reddish with bold white streaks, neck and upper body dull to bright rufous-brown, streaked with fine dark and white lines.  The face is mainly white except for the rufous forehead, white lores and a thin partial white eye-ring beneath the eye; and sometimes a rufous fore-supercilium. Black and white ear coverts separate the dark head parts from the off-white chin and throat. The tail is dark grey-brown with off-white shafts and light brown fringes. Upperwings are also dark grey-brown, but with prominent white shafts and narrow rufous-brown fringes to the secondary coverts and tertials; and fine light brown edges to the other remiges, producing rufous-brown patches when the wing is folded. The underbody is white with buff-brown wash on the flanks through to the legs and underside of tail. The bill is light grey to blue-grey with a darker grey culmen, and the iris is dark to olive-brown. The legs and feet are purplish to dark grey.


Female
Identical to males but the flanks are a brighter rufous-brown, and not as distinctly pale compared to the upperparts. The legs may be a slightly paler grey with stronger purplish tinge. Females are slightly smaller overall and have a finer bill.


Nestling, fledgling and juvenile
Nestlings are naked with dark grey down on the head and wings. The bare skin is mostly pink with dark blue-grey skin around the eyes. Newly fledged birds have lemon or yellow gape-flanges. Partial moult begins soon after fledging, producing similar patterns to adult male but upperparts are much duller and browner, with less distinct streaking and facial patterns. They are fluffier which can give a mottled appearance around the face and ear coverts. The eye is olive and the bill is light grey, lacking the dark culmen and brown tip seen in the adult male. In young birds the skull is not fully pneumatised.


Systematics and taxonomy
The Eyrean grasswren was originally described by John Gould in 1875, who named it Amytis goyderi. Gould assumed it was related to the western grasswren (A. textilis) due to similarities in plumage characters. During the 20th century, various authors placed it in clades derived from either A. textilis or A. striatus, considering it closely related to either A. modestus or A. striatus. With the development of molecular studies, it was found that A. goyderi belongs in a clade with A. ballarae and A. purnelli which appears to have evolved from a common ancestor with A. textilis.


Evolutionary history
There are two possible explanations for grasswren diversity in Central Australia: Either they originated there, or they colonised it. The close relationships between A. goyderi and other central taxa indicate that they arose there in the last 100000 years as glaciation events influenced the vegetation structures in Central Australia. A. goyderi is one of very few avian species to evolve in a minor ecological refuge.


Distribution
A. goyderi has a patchy, restricted distribution, found only in dune fields of the Simpson and Strzelecki deserts of Central Australia. These deserts are located in the Birdsville Structural Basin, an enormous drainage basin centring on Lake Eyre. Most populations are found in South Australia from north of Cameron Corner to Witjira National Park; with some in SW Queensland and the Northern Territory. It is likely that populations are plastic, being most abundant when canegrass is plentiful and withdrawing to refuges during drought.


Habitat
The Eyrean grasswren habitat consists of sandhill canegrass (Zygochloa paradoxa) tussocks on large, loosely sanded dune crests and slopes; as well as in speargrass (Aristida holathera), spinifex (Triodia spp.) and dune pea (Swainsona rigida) where it grows among Z. paradoxa. Typical landscapes consist of tussocks ranging from 1–4m high and 2–3m in diameter, spaced well apart and with bare ground between them. The birds rarely stray from dune slopes; but are sometimes observed in swales.


Behaviour
No extensive surveys of Eyrean grasswren behaviour have been undertaken, but it is thought to be sedentary. They are usually found in singles and pairs, or small groups of up to ten. It is cryptic, remaining hidden within Z. paradoxa tussocks, and is difficult to flush. Flight is rare, but when flushed the birds bound with wings half spread between tussock clumps or fly short distances of up to 10m with the tail trailing. They use a distinctive "half running, half flying" movement low to the ground, bounding and flying for short distances of 40–220 cm. Movements are quick and furtive with the tail usually held cocked. They sometimes perch low (c. 1 m) on grevillea branches, canegrass or other shrubs.


Diet
Prey consists of about equal amounts of vegetation and invertebrates. Specimen stomach contents include seeds from grasses Z. paradoxa  and Aristida holathera, as well as remnants of numerous invertebrate species. Individuals move with small hops while foraging between clumps of spinifex (Triodia spp.) and within clumps of dune pea (Swainsona rigida). It will sometimes skip backwards and shuffle the feet to uncover food items in the sand.


Reproduction
Very little is known about the mating habits of A. goyderi. No information exists on laying or incubation durations. Nests containing nestlings and eggs have been located from July to September and dependent fledglings from May to September. Clutches are 2–3 broadly oval eggs with slight variations in shape. It is assumed to be monogamous and to breed throughout the range.
Females are responsible for nest construction, which takes about one week to complete. Nests are wedged into tussock stems close to the ground (usually <1m height) in clumps of Z. paradoxa and Australian boxthorn (Lychan austral). The nest consists of a truncated cup or dome of varying dimensions made from grasses such as A. holathera with Z. paradoxa strands woven in to the base, while an inner cup consists of finer grass and sometimes downy plant material or spider silk. Nests are well spaced but may be more densely aggregated in patches of good habitat.


Vocalisations
Contact calls are made throughout the day, usually given as call and response. Adults of both sexes sing, which may function in territorial defence or maintaining group cohesion. Songs are performed from low perches or on the ground, and may increase in frequency during the breeding season. The songs are "distinctive", consisting of "beautiful silvery cadences, pip trills and long bursting staccatos" which may sometimes be meshed together in excited bursts. These may be heard up to 100m away, while the quieter contact calls can be heard from 30–40m. Alarm calls are high and sharp, and are louder than the contact call.


Conservation
A. goyderi was listed by the IUCN as threatened in 1988, and as special concern by the Royal Australian Ornithologist's Union in 1992 as further research was required. The IUCN currently lists it as least concern since the species is not thought to be declining and probably fluctuates due to seasonal cycles.
Threats include any species or processes affecting the quality of sandhill canegrass habitat such as erosion driven by grazing rabbits, camels and livestock.


Ornithological history
The first record of A. goyderi came from an expedition by J.W.Lewis into the arid northern regions of South Australia in 1874, resulting in the collection of six specimens obtained near the Macumba river on Lake Eyre. Two of these specimens were forwarded by the curator of the South Australian Museum F.W.Andrews, to John Gould who described them in 1875. These were later given to the British Museum of Natural History, however the remainder were lost. A third specimen was found in the Dobroyde collection housed at the Australian Museum in Sydney; although it is now thought that this is not one of the missing type specimens. No further specimens were obtained until a century after Lewis' expedition when two were acquired in Qld in 1976. A second expedition later that year resulted in 19 specimens, 3 nests and 2 eggs. Prior to then, numerous specimens were misidentified.
In the time since May's observations at Poeppel's Corner the species has been located many times. However, due to the cryptic nature of the species and the remoteness of its distribution very little research into the behavioural habits has been undertaken. Some attempts to trap the bird failed as they are so small, they were able to pass through mist nets.


References


External links
Gould's presentation in Annals and Magazine of Natural History
Atlas of Living Australia records for Amytornis goyderi

uncommon bird [[Endemism|endemic]] to arid regions of [[Central Australia]].<ref name="Schodde and Christidis 1987">{{cite journal|last1=Schodde|first1=R.|last2=Christidis|first2=L.|title=Short Andrews in 1874 around the [[Macumba River]] at [[Lake Eyre]], and named after the South Australian Surveyor General [[George Woodroffe Goyder]].<ref name="Morgan et al." /><ref name=Campbell>{{cite journal|last1=Campbell|first1=A.J.|title=The Long-lost Eyrean Grass Wren|journal=Emu|date=1923|volume=23|issue=2|page=81|doi=10.1071/mu923081|bibcode=1923EmuAO..23...81C |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/part/397988 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Condon|first1=H.T.|title=Notes on the birds of South Australia: Occurrence, distribution, and taxonomy|journal=South Australian Ornithology|date=1951|volume=20|pages=26–28}}</ref><ref name=Hindwood>{{cite journal|last1=Hindwood|first1=K.A.|title=The Eyrean Grasswren (Amytornis goyderi) A Third Specimen|journal=Emu|date=1945|volume=44|issue=4|pages=321–323|url=http://www.publish.csiro.au/?act=view_file&file_id=MU944321.pdf|doi=10.1071/mu944321|bibcode=1945EmuAO..44..321H }}</ref><ref name=May>{{cite journal|last1=May|first1=I.A.|title=Recent re-discovery of the Eyrean grasswren Amytornis goyderi (Gould, 1875)|journal=Emu|date=1977|volume=77|pages=230–231|doi=10.1071/mu9770230}}</ref><ref name="Parker et al. 1978">{{cite journal|last1=Parker|first1=S.A.|last2=May|first2=I.A.|last3=Head|first3=W.|title=Some observations on the Eyrean grasswren|journal=Records of the South Australian Museum|date=1978|volume=17|issue=24|pages=361–371}}</ref><ref name="Schodde 1982" />

==Description==
[[File:Eyrean Grasswren female in Sturt National Park.jpg|thumb|left|A female Eyrean grasswren.]]
At 14–16.5 cm in length, ''Amytornis goyderi'' is the smallest [[grasswren]].<ref name="Higgins et al" /> It has a deep, finch-like bill. There are some minor differences between sexes, and between populations across the distribution.
[[File:Amytis goyderi - The Birds of New Guinea (cropped).jpg|thumb|left|200px|Illustration by [[John Gould]] and [[William Matthew Hart|W. Hart]]]]

===Adult male===
The head is reddish with bold white streaks, neck and upper body dull to bright rufous-brown, streaked with fine dark and white lines.  The face is mainly white except for the rufous forehead, white [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]] and a thin partial white eye-ring beneath the eye; and sometimes a rufous fore-[[supercilium]]. Black and white ear [[Covert feather|coverts]] separate the dark head parts from the off-white chin and throat. The tail is dark grey-brown with off-white shafts and light brown fringes. Upperwings are also dark grey-brown, but with prominent white shafts and narrow rufous-brown fringes to the secondary [[Covert feather|coverts]] and [[tertials]]; and fine light brown edges to the other [[remiges]], producing rufous-brown patches when the wing is folded. The underbody is white with buff-brown wash on the flanks through to the legs and underside of tail. The bill is light grey to blue-grey with a darker grey [[Culmen (bird)|culmen]], and the [[Iris (anatomy)|iris]] is dark to olive-brown. The legs and feet are purplish to dark grey.<ref name="Higgins et al" /><ref name="Morgan et al." /><ref name="Parker et al. 1978" /><ref name=Gould>{{cite journal|last1=Gould|first1=John|title=Further contributions to the Ornithology of Australia|journal=Annals and Magazine of Natural History|series=Series 4|volume=16|issue=94|pages=285–287|url=http://biostor.org/reference/59934|doi=10.1080/00222937508681849|year=1875}}</ref><ref name="Rowley and Russell 1997">{{cite book|last1=Rowley|first1=I.|last2=Russell|first2=E.|title=Bird Families of the World. IV. Fairy-wrens and Grasswrens.|date=1997|publisher=Oxford University Press|pages=233–236}}</ref>

===Female===
Identical to males but the flanks are a brighter rufous-brown, and not as distinctly pale compared to the upperparts. The legs may be a slightly paler grey with stronger purplish tinge. Females are slightly smaller overall and have a finer bill.<ref name="Higgins et al" /><ref name=Gould />

===Nestling, fledgling and juvenile===
Nestlings are naked with dark grey down on the head and wings.<ref name="Morgan et al." /> The bare skin is mostly pink with dark blue-grey skin around the eyes.<ref name="Higgins et al" /> Newly [[fledge]]d birds have lemon or yellow [[beak|gape-flanges]]. Partial [[moulting|moult]] begins soon after fledging, producing similar patterns to adult male but upperparts are much duller and browner, with less distinct streaking and facial patterns. They are fluffier which can give a mottled appearance around the face and ear [[Covert feather|coverts]]. The eye is olive and the bill is light grey, lacking the dark culmen and brown tip seen in the adult male. In young birds the skull is not fully [[Skeletal pneumaticity|pneumatised]].<ref name="Parker et al. 1978" />

== Systematics and taxonomy ==
The Eyrean grasswren was originally described by [[John Gould]] in 1875, who named it ''Amytis goyderi''.<ref name=Gould /> Gould assumed it was related to the [[western grasswren]] (''A. textilis'') due to similarities in plumage characters. During the 20th century, various authors placed it in clades derived from either ''A. textilis'' or [[Amytornis striatus|''A. striatus'']],<ref name="Schodde 1982">{{cite book|last1=Schodde|first1=R.|title=The Fairy-wrens: A Monograph of the Maluridae.|date=1982|publisher=Lansdowne Editions|location=Melbourne}}</ref><ref name=Keast>{{cite journal|last1=Keast|first1=A.|title=Speciation in the genus Amytornis Stejneger (Passeres: Muscicapidae, Malurinae) in Australia.|journal=Australian Journal of Zoology|date=1958|volume=6|issue=1|pages=33–52|doi=10.1071/zo9580033}}</ref><ref name="Parker 1972">{{cite journal|last1=Parker|first1=S.A.|title=Remarks on distribution and taxonomy of the grass wrens Amytornis textilis, modestus and purnelli.|journal=Emu|date=1972|volume=72|issue=4|pages=157–166|url=http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU972157|doi=10.1071/mu972157|bibcode=1972EmuAO..72..157P |url-access=subscription}}</ref> considering it closely related to either [[Amytornis modestus|''A. modestus'']]<ref name=Keast /> or ''A. striatus''.<ref name="Schodde 1982" /><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Ford|first1=J.|title=Speciation in Australian birds adapted to arid habitats|journal=Emu|date=1974|volume=74|issue=3|pages=161–178|url=http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU974161|doi=10.1071/mu974161|bibcode=1974EmuAO..74..161F |url-access=subscription}}</ref> With the development of molecular studies, it was found that ''A. goyderi'' belongs in a clade with [[Amytornis ballarae|''A. ballarae'']] and [[Amytornis purnelli|''A. purnelli'']] which appears to have evolved from a common ancestor with ''A. textilis''.<ref name="Christidis et al 2010" /><ref name="Christidis 1999">{{cite journal|last1=Christidis|first1=L.|title=Evolution and biogeography of the Australian grasswrens, Amytornis (Aves: Maluridae): biochemical perspectives.|journal=Australian Journal of Zoology|date=1999|volume=47|issue=2|pages=113–124|doi=10.1071/zo98054}}</ref>

===Evolutionary history===
There are two possible explanations for grasswren diversity in Central Australia: Either they originated there,<ref name=Keast /> or they colonised it.<ref name="Christidis 1999" /> The close relationships between ''A. goyderi'' and other central taxa indicate that they arose there in the last 100000 years as glaciation events influenced the vegetation structures in [[Central Australia]]. ''A. goyderi'' is one of very few avian species to evolve in a minor ecological refuge.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Ford|first1=J.|title= Minor Isolates and Minor Geographical Barriers in Avian Speciation in Continental Australia.|journal=Emu|date=1987|volume=87|issue=2|pages=90–102|url=http://www.publish.csiro.au/?paper=MU9870090|doi=10.1071/mu9870090|bibcode=1987EmuAO..87...90F |url-access=subscription}}</ref>

==Distribution==
''A. goyderi'' has a patchy,<ref name="Schodde 1982" /> restricted distribution,<ref name="Christidis 1999" /> found only in dune fields of the [[Simpson Desert|Simpson]] and [[Strzelecki Desert|Strzelecki]] deserts of Central Australia. These deserts are located in the Birdsville Structural Basin, an enormous drainage basin centring on [[Lake Eyre]].<ref name="Schodde and Christidis 1987" /> Most populations are found in [[South Australia]] from north of [[Cameron Corner, Queensland|Cameron Corner]] to [[Witjira National Park]];<ref name="Higgins et al" /> with some in SW [[Queensland]]<ref name=May /> and the [[Northern Territory]].<ref name="Higgins et al" /> It is likely that populations are plastic, being most abundant when [[Zygochloa|canegrass]] is plentiful and withdrawing to refuges during drought.<ref name="Schodde 1982" />

==Habitat==
The Eyrean grasswren habitat consists of [[Zygochloa|sandhill canegrass]] (''Zygochloa paradoxa'') tussocks on large, loosely sanded dune crests and slopes; as well as in speargrass (''[[Aristida]] holathera''), spinifex (''[[Triodia (grass)|Triodia]]'' spp.) and dune pea (''[[Swainsona]] rigida'') where it grows among ''Z. paradoxa''.<ref name="Higgins et al" /><ref name="Parker et al. 1978" /> Typical landscapes consist of [[Tussock (grass)|tussocks]] ranging from 1–4m high and 2–3m in diameter, spaced well apart and with bare ground between them.<ref name="Higgins et al" /><ref name="Morgan et al." /> The birds rarely stray from dune slopes;<ref name="Parker et al. 1978" /> but are sometimes observed in [[Swale (landform)|swale]]s.<ref name="Higgins et al" />

==Behaviour==
No extensive surveys of Eyrean grasswren behaviour have been undertaken, but it is thought to be sedentary.<ref name="Higgins et al" /> They are usually found in singles and pairs, or small groups of up to ten. It is cryptic, remaining hidden within ''Z. paradoxa'' tussocks, and is difficult to flush.<ref name="Morgan et al." /><ref name="Parker et al. 1978" /> Flight is rare,<ref name="Schodde 1982" /> but when flushed the birds bound with wings half spread between tussock clumps<ref name="Morgan et al." /> or fly short distances of up to 10m with the tail trailing.<ref name="Higgins et al" /> They use a distinctive "half running, half flying" movement low to the ground, bounding and flying for short distances of 40–220 cm. Movements are quick and furtive with the tail usually held cocked. They sometimes perch low (c. 1 m) on [[grevillea]] branches,<ref name="Parker et al. 1978" /> [[Zygochloa|canegrass]] or other shrubs.<ref name="Higgins et al" />

==Diet==
Prey consists of about equal amounts of vegetation and invertebrates.<ref name="Higgins et al" /> Specimen stomach contents include seeds from grasses [[Zygochloa|''Z. paradoxa'']]  and [[Aristida|''Aristida holathera'']], as well as remnants of numerous invertebrate species.<ref name="Parker et al. 1978" /><ref>{{cite book|last1=Barker|first1=R.|last2=Vestjens|first2=W.|title=The food of Australian birds. II. Passerines.|date=1984|publisher=CSIRO|location=Melbourne|pages=130–131}}</ref> Individuals move with small hops while foraging between clumps of spinifex ([[Triodia (grass)|''Triodia'']] spp.) and within clumps of dune pea (''[[Swainsona]] rigida''). It will sometimes skip backwards and shuffle the feet to uncover food items in the sand.<ref name="Higgins et al" />

==Reproduction==
Very little is known about the mating habits of ''A. goyderi''.<ref name="Higgins et al" /> No information exists on laying or [[Egg incubation|incubation]] durations. Nests containing nestlings and eggs have been located from July<ref name="Higgins et al" /> to September<ref name="Morgan et al." /><ref name="Parker et al. 1978" /> and dependent fledglings from May to September.<ref name="Higgins et al" /> Clutches are 2–3 broadly oval eggs with slight variations in shape.<ref name="Higgins et al" /><ref name="Parker et al. 1978" /> It is assumed to be monogamous and to breed throughout the range.<ref name="Higgins et al" />

Females are responsible for nest construction, which takes about one week to complete.<ref name="Higgins et al" /> Nests are wedged into tussock stems close to the ground (usually <1m height) in clumps of Z. paradoxa and Australian boxthorn (''Lychan austral'').<ref name="Higgins et al" /><ref name="Morgan et al." /> The nest consists of a truncated cup or dome of varying dimensions made from grasses such as ''A. holathera'' with ''Z. paradoxa'' strands woven in to the base, while an inner cup consists of finer grass and sometimes downy plant material or spider silk.<ref name="Higgins et al" /><ref name="Morgan et al." /><ref name="Parker et al. 1978" /> Nests are well spaced but may be more densely aggregated in patches of good habitat.<ref name="Higgins et al" />

==Vocalisations==
Contact calls are made throughout the day, usually given as call and response.<ref name="Higgins et al" /> Adults of both sexes sing, which may function in territorial defence or maintaining group cohesion. Songs are performed from low perches or on the ground, and may increase in frequency during the breeding season. The songs are "distinctive", consisting of "beautiful silvery cadences, pip trills and long bursting staccatos"<ref name="Higgins et al" /> which may sometimes be meshed together in excited bursts.<ref name="Parker et al. 1978" /> These may be heard up to 100m away, while the quieter contact calls can be heard from 30–40m.<ref name="Higgins et al" /> Alarm calls are high and sharp, and are louder than the contact call.

==Conservation==
''A. goyderi'' was listed by the [[IUCN]] as threatened in 1988,<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /> and as special concern by the Royal Australian Ornithologist's Union in 1992<ref>{{cite book|last1=Garnett|first1=S.|title=Threatened and extinct birds of Australia|date=1992|publisher=Royal Australasian Ornithologists Union and Australian National Parks and Wildlife Service|location=Melbourne}}</ref> as further research was required.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Reid|first1=J.|last2=Fleming|first2=M.|title=The conservation status of birds in arid Australia|journal=The Rangeland Journal|date=1992|volume=14|issue=2|pages=65–91|doi=10.1071/rj9920065 |bibcode=1992RangJ..14...65R }}</ref> The IUCN currently lists it as [[least concern]] since the species is not thought to be declining and probably fluctuates due to seasonal cycles.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" />

Threats include any species or processes affecting the quality of [[Zygochloa|sandhill canegrass]] habitat such as erosion driven by grazing rabbits, camels and livestock.<ref name="Higgins et al" />

==Ornithological history==
The first record of ''A. goyderi'' came from an expedition by J.W.Lewis into the arid northern regions of South Australia in 1874, resulting in the collection of six specimens obtained near the Macumba river on Lake Eyre.<ref name="Higgins et al" /><ref name=Hindwood /><ref name=May /> Two of these specimens were forwarded by the curator of the South Australian Museum F.W.Andrews, to [[John Gould]] who described them in 1875. These were later given to the British Museum of Natural History, however the remainder were lost.<ref name=Campbell /> A third specimen was found in the Dobroyde collection housed at the Australian Museum in Sydney;<ref name=Hindwood /> although it is now thought that this is not one of the missing type specimens.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Longmore|first1=N.W.|title=Type specimens in the Australian Museum|journal=Technical Reports of the Australian Museum|date=1991|volume=4|doi=10.3853/j.1031-8062.4.1991.75|doi-access=free}}</ref> No further specimens were obtained until a century after Lewis' expedition when two were acquired in Qld in 1976.<ref name=May /> A second expedition later that year resulted in 19 specimens, 3 nests and 2 eggs.<ref name="Parker et al. 1978" /> Prior to then, numerous specimens were misidentified.<ref name="Parker 1972" /><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Black|first1=A.A.|last2=Longmore|first2=N.W.|title=Notes on Grasswren Eggs in Len Harvey's Collection|journal=Australian Field Ornithology|date=20 August 2020 |volume=26|issue=4 |pages=132–141|url=http://search.informit.com.au/documentSummary;dn=525599834865326;res=IELHSS}}</ref>
 
In the time since May's observations at Poeppel's Corner the species has been located many times. However, due to the cryptic nature of the species and the remoteness of its distribution very little research into the behavioural habits has been undertaken.<ref name="Higgins et al" /> Some attempts to trap the bird failed as they are so small, they were able to pass through mist nets.<ref name="Morgan et al." />
